question: What drew you to Development Studies rather than, say, Economics or Politics on its own?
lookingFor: A genuine account of why the interdisciplinary, problem-centred nature of Development Studies is the draw — not just an interest in poverty or global issues.

bullets: Name the interdisciplinary appeal — why one discipline alone felt insufficient
Connect to a concrete experience, place, or question that made it click
Show awareness of what Development Studies does that its constituent disciplines do not
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I kept running into the limits of single disciplines when I tried to understand something as layered as why a community stays poor despite economic growth nearby. Economics gave me models but not the politics; Politics gave me institutions but not the lived experience. Development Studies is the only frame that holds all of that at once and treats the messiness as the point, not a complication. I spent a summer volunteering with a rural sanitation project and left with more questions than I arrived with — this course felt like the right place to take those questions seriously.

commonMistakes: Saying 'I want to help people' without any analytical framing
Treating Development Studies as simply Economics with a conscience
Naming no specific experience or intellectual turning point
followUps: Can you give an example of a question you couldn't answer with one discipline alone?

question: Why do you want to study Development Studies at this institution specifically?
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine research — a named module, research cluster, or methodological approach that connects to the applicant's interests.

bullets: Cite something specific — a module, a faculty member's work, a research centre
Connect it to your own intellectual or practical interest
Show you understand what distinguishes this course from similar ones elsewhere
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Your department's emphasis on participatory action research stood out to me immediately, because so much of what I've read critiques top-down development models that design interventions without the community. The module on feminist political economy also caught my attention — I've been reading around gender and land rights in sub-Saharan Africa and I'd like to study that properly, not as a side topic. I applied here because the course treats methodology as political, not just technical, and I think that's the right starting point.

commonMistakes: Generic praise that would fit any university offering the subject
Mentioning only rankings or reputation
Confusing this department's approach with a different institution's programme
followUps: Which module are you most nervous about, and why?

question: How would you define 'development'? Is it a helpful concept?
lookingFor: Awareness that 'development' is contested — not just economic growth — and the ability to hold multiple definitions critically.

bullets: Acknowledge that 'development' has multiple, often contested meanings
Distinguish at least two frameworks — e.g. GDP-centred vs. capabilities vs. post-development critiques
Take a position while acknowledging its limitations
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Development is one of those words that sounds obvious until you pull on it. The dominant definition has been economic — rising GDP, industrialisation, modernisation — but that misses how unevenly growth distributes and what people actually value. Amartya Sen's capabilities approach is more persuasive to me: development as expanding the real freedoms people have to live the life they want to live. But there's a deeper critique — the post-development school argues the whole concept is a Western frame that pathologises the Global South. I find that critique useful as a corrective even if I'm not ready to abandon the concept entirely, because communities are still experiencing very real deprivation and they need frameworks to address it.

commonMistakes: Treating development as synonymous with economic growth without question
Listing frameworks without engaging with what they actually argue
Refusing to take any position and hiding behind 'it's complicated'
followUps: Which definition do you think is most useful in practice, and for whom?

question: Tell us about a book or article you've read recently that shaped how you think about development.
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual engagement — the ability to summarise an argument, agree or push back, and connect it to the field.

bullets: Name a specific text and summarise its central argument accurately
Explain what it changed or challenged in your thinking
Show you can critically assess it — agree, disagree, or complicate
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Ha-Joon Chang's Bad Samaritans last year, which argues that wealthy countries are pulling up the ladder behind them by insisting developing nations adopt free trade and deregulation — policies those same countries didn't use during their own industrialisation. What struck me is how historically grounded the argument is; it's hard to dismiss. Where I'm less sure is whether the state-led industrialisation models he points to are replicable in the current global context, given how different trade and capital flows are now. It pushed me to be much more sceptical of policy prescriptions that come dressed as economic consensus.

commonMistakes: Naming a book without being able to summarise its argument
Only agreeing with the text — no critical engagement
Choosing a text so well-known it sounds like a syllabus pick rather than genuine reading
followUps: Is there a counter-argument to that book you find persuasive?

question: Why do you think global poverty persists despite decades of development efforts?
lookingFor: Multi-causal thinking that goes beyond aid or corruption to structural and historical factors.

bullets: Identify at least two structural factors — historical, institutional, or systemic
Avoid reducing the answer to aid effectiveness or individual-country failings
Show awareness of how global systems (trade, finance, debt) play a role
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think a lot of the narrative focuses on what poor countries lack — institutions, governance, capital — without asking how the global system shapes those conditions. Colonial extraction structured many economies as commodity exporters in ways that persisted after independence. Trade rules still favour richer countries, intellectual property regimes restrict technology transfer, and debt repayment diverts public spending. I'm not dismissing domestic governance — it genuinely matters — but putting it first lets the international architecture off the hook too easily. Persistent poverty is partly a design feature of a system that benefits others, and that's the uncomfortable part.

commonMistakes: Attributing poverty solely to corruption or poor governance
Treating aid as the main solution without questioning structural causes
Ignoring colonial history and its long-run effects
followUps: Does that framing lead anywhere practically, or is it just critique?

question: There's a long-running debate about whether foreign aid does more harm than good. Where do you stand?
lookingFor: Familiarity with the aid effectiveness debate — Sachs vs. Moyo-style positions — and a reasoned, nuanced view.

bullets: Acknowledge both sides of the debate with specific arguments, not just 'some say yes, some say no'
Distinguish between types of aid — emergency relief, budget support, tied aid, etc.
Land on a position while noting what evidence would change your mind
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The debate is often framed as Jeffrey Sachs versus Dambisa Moyo — big push aid versus aid dependency — and I think both capture something real but neither is the whole story. Emergency humanitarian aid in a crisis is a different thing from decades of budget support that bypasses state capacity-building. The strongest critique of aid isn't that money is wasted, it's that it can relieve pressure on governments to be accountable to their own citizens and can distort local markets. But I'd push back on the idea that cutting aid is therefore the answer — the question is what design, conditionality, and accountability look like. I'm drawn to the evidence-based approach: ask which specific interventions improve which outcomes, not whether 'aid works' in the abstract.

commonMistakes: Taking a strong position with no awareness of the counter-arguments
Treating all aid as a single category
Confusing foreign direct investment with official development assistance
followUps: What evidence would convince you to change your view?

question: Tell us about an experience — volunteering, travel, work, or otherwise — that made you want to study this subject seriously.
lookingFor: A specific, reflective account that shows critical thinking rather than gap-year romanticism.

bullets: Describe the experience concisely and specifically
Explain what it made you question or rethink, not just feel
Connect it to a concept or question you now want to study
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent three weeks with a development NGO running a microfinance programme in rural Bangladesh during my gap year. What I expected was a feel-good experience; what I got was a lot of discomfort. The loan officers were under pressure to hit repayment targets in a way that sometimes meant households cut food spending rather than miss a payment. I came back wanting to understand how well-intentioned interventions produce these outcomes — the political economy of implementation, not just the design. That question is directly what I want to study here.

commonMistakes: Describing what you did without reflecting on what it made you think
A narrative that centres your own feelings over what you observed
Using the experience to signal virtue rather than genuine intellectual engagement
followUps: What would you do differently if you were designing that programme?

question: What do you make of the Sustainable Development Goals? Are they useful?
lookingFor: Familiarity with the SDGs beyond listing them, plus critical reflection on their strengths and limits.

bullets: Show you know what the SDGs are and name at least one or two specifically
Identify a genuine strength — the breadth of consensus, measurability, political leverage
Engage with at least one serious critique — too many goals, weak accountability, universality tension
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the SDGs are a genuinely useful political tool even if they're imperfect analytically. Having 193 countries agree on 17 goals creates a shared vocabulary for advocacy and lets civil society hold governments to something. I find goals like SDG 10 on inequality particularly important because it acknowledges that growth without distribution isn't enough. The criticisms I take seriously are that 169 targets is too sprawling to prioritise, there's no enforcement mechanism when countries miss them, and some goals paper over tensions — you can't always maximise economic growth and environmental sustainability simultaneously. But I'd rather have an imperfect framework that's politically alive than a perfect one that no government touches.

commonMistakes: Reciting the number of goals without any critical reflection
Dismissing the SDGs entirely without acknowledging their political function
Confusing the SDGs with the earlier Millennium Development Goals
followUps: Which single SDG would you prioritise if you had to, and why?

question: How does climate change complicate the traditional development agenda?
lookingFor: Understanding that climate change is not just an environmental issue — it interacts with inequality, justice, and development pathways in ways that require Development Studies thinking.

bullets: Note the injustice dimension — lowest emitters are often most affected
Explain the development pathway tension — can countries industrialise as others did?
Show awareness of adaptation vs. mitigation as a live policy debate
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The core tension is a justice one: the countries that contributed least to historical emissions are often the most exposed to the consequences — coastal Bangladesh, the Sahel, small island states. That raises hard questions about who owes what to whom, which is as much a political as a scientific problem. There's also the development pathway question: rich countries industrialised on fossil fuels, and now they're telling poorer nations to leapfrog straight to renewables. That's easier to fund and technically possible, but it requires massive capital transfers that aren't happening at scale. I think the climate-development nexus explodes the idea that you can do development and environment separately — they have to be designed together.

commonMistakes: Treating climate change purely as an environmental issue with no development dimension
Ignoring the justice and responsibility question
Presenting adaptation and mitigation as identical rather than different policy responses
followUps: Who do you think should bear the cost of climate adaptation in the Global South?

question: Why is gender such a central lens in Development Studies?
lookingFor: Understanding that gender is not a side topic but a structural organising dimension of economic and social life — and familiarity with at least one framework or critique.

bullets: Explain that gender shapes access to resources, labour, and voice — not just individual outcomes
Reference a framework, concept, or well-known intervention (e.g. WID/GAD, Sen's agency, microfinance critique)
Note tensions — e.g. instrumentalisation of women's empowerment for growth targets
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Gender is central because it structures how people access land, credit, education, and political voice — it's not a demographic variable, it's a power relation. The field moved from Women in Development, which added women to existing frameworks, toward Gender and Development, which asks how those frameworks themselves are gendered. One tension I find intellectually interesting is when women's empowerment gets justified instrumentally — 'invest in women because it improves GDP' — rather than as a rights claim. That framing risks reducing women to a development input. I'm also interested in how gender intersects with class and ethnicity, because the category 'women' obscures huge variation in vulnerability.

commonMistakes: Treating gender as synonymous with women's issues rather than a relational power structure
No awareness of the WID/GAD shift or any conceptual framework
Missing the instrumentalisation critique
followUps: Can you think of a development intervention that claimed to empower women but had mixed results?

question: How much does colonialism explain contemporary underdevelopment?
lookingFor: Engagement with dependency theory or related arguments, a sense of historical causation, and the ability to hold complexity — not a monocausal answer.

bullets: Argue that colonial structures — extraction, boundary drawing, institutions — have long-run effects
Reference at least one theoretical tradition (dependency theory, historical institutionalism, Acemoglu/Robinson type arguments)
Acknowledge what colonialism doesn't explain — variation between former colonies, post-independence choices
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Colonial history is indispensable for understanding underdevelopment, but I resist treating it as a complete explanation. Dependency theorists like Walter Rodney in How Europe Underdeveloped Africa make a powerful case that colonial economies were deliberately structured to extract rather than industrialise — and those structures didn't evaporate at independence. Acemoglu and Robinson's work on extractive versus inclusive institutions points in a similar direction: the institutions colonisers built for extraction tended to persist. But colonial history doesn't explain the variation between former colonies — South Korea and the DRC were both colonised; their post-independence trajectories differ enormously. So I think colonialism sets the initial conditions and creates path dependencies, but what happens next also depends on domestic politics, geography, and agency.

commonMistakes: Either dismissing colonialism as irrelevant or treating it as the only explanation
No familiarity with any theoretical framework beyond a general 'colonialism was bad'
Ignoring variation between former colonies
followUps: Which former colony do you think has managed to escape that path dependency most successfully, and how?

question: Development researchers use both quantitative and qualitative methods. Which do you think is more important for understanding poverty, and why?
lookingFor: Understanding that both approaches generate different kinds of knowledge — and that the choice is not about which is 'better' but what questions each can answer.

bullets: Articulate what each method does well — quantitative for scale and comparison, qualitative for meaning and process
Argue that the question determines the method, not the other way round
Demonstrate awareness of mixed-methods approaches or participatory research
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd resist the framing that one is more important — I think they're answering different questions. Large-scale household surveys can tell you how many people live below a poverty line and how that changes over time; that's essential for tracking progress. But they tell you almost nothing about why — what poverty feels like, what trade-offs households make daily, how agency and shame and aspiration interact. Qualitative work gets at that, but it's hard to generalise from. The most compelling research I've read combines both: randomised trials that measure impact and ethnographic work that explains the mechanism. If anything, I think Development Studies has historically undervalued qualitative methods because 'rigour' became conflated with quantification.

commonMistakes: Arguing for one approach with no engagement with what the other does well
Treating qualitative work as less rigorous by definition
No awareness of participatory or mixed-methods traditions
followUps: What's one research question where you think qualitative methods would be the only appropriate tool?

question: What role should the state play in economic development?
lookingFor: Awareness of the debate between market-led and state-led development — the Washington Consensus vs. developmental state arguments — and a reasoned position.

bullets: Reference the Washington Consensus and its critique
Point to developmental state examples — East Asia — and what made them work or not
Take a position on what the state must do that markets won't
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Washington Consensus answer — liberalise, privatise, deregulate — has been fairly thoroughly discredited empirically, especially after the 1990s crises it couldn't explain. The East Asian developmental states offer a different model: South Korea and Taiwan used industrial policy, subsidised credit, and conditional protection to build competitive industries, then integrated into global markets. The state coordinated investment in ways private actors wouldn't, because the returns were too long-term or too diffuse. I think the honest answer is that markets are terrible at providing certain things — health, basic infrastructure, education, long-run industrial capacity — and the state has to step in. The question is always capacity and accountability: a capable, embedded but autonomous state can drive development; a captured state captures the rents.

commonMistakes: Treating markets and states as always in opposition rather than complementary
No familiarity with the developmental state literature or Washington Consensus debate
Ignoring variation in state capacity across countries
followUps: Can the developmental state model be replicated today, given WTO rules on industrial policy?

question: Some scholars argue that 'development' itself is a harmful concept that should be abandoned. How do you respond to that?
lookingFor: Familiarity with the post-development school (Escobar, Sachs) and the ability to engage seriously — neither dismissing it nor accepting it uncritically.

bullets: Show you know what the post-development critique actually argues — not a straw man
Identify what's compelling about it — the discourse critique, the homogenising effect
Explain what's problematic about abandoning the concept entirely
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The post-development school, associated with Arturo Escobar and Wolfgang Sachs, argues that development is a discourse invented by the West that pathologises non-Western societies, creates 'underdevelopment' as a problem it then claims to fix, and erases local knowledge and diversity. I think the discourse critique is genuinely important — how we name problems shapes what solutions seem possible, and 'underdevelopment' has often served as a justification for external intervention. Where I get stuck is the political implication: if development is abandoned as a concept, what frame do communities experiencing hunger, preventable disease, or landlessness use to make claims? The post-development critique is strongest as a corrective to hubris; I'm not convinced it's a programme.

commonMistakes: Dismissing post-development as radical without engaging with what it actually argues
Accepting it fully without noting the practical problem it leaves
Confusing post-development with post-colonialism, which is related but distinct
followUps: Are there communities where you think the post-development critique is most applicable?

question: Is it more urgent to address inequality between countries or inequality within countries?
lookingFor: Understanding that both dimensions of inequality matter, with an ability to argue for a position while acknowledging the trade-offs.

bullets: Distinguish clearly between inter- and intra-national inequality
Note that within-country inequality has risen even in fast-growing economies
Argue for a position that acknowledges interdependence — they're often produced by the same forces
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Global income gaps between rich and poor countries have narrowed somewhat over the last thirty years, partly because of East and South Asian growth. But within-country inequality has risen in many of those same fast-growing economies — China, India, Brazil — so the aggregate picture hides what's happening to distribution. I find within-country inequality increasingly urgent because it drives the political instability and social breakdown that makes sustained development harder. Branko Milanovic's elephant chart showed that middle-income people in rich countries and the poorest in developing countries were the biggest losers from globalisation — which means the two dimensions are connected, not separate agendas. I'd resist the idea that we have to choose: the forces creating both are often the same.

commonMistakes: Treating the two as completely separate problems
No familiarity with the empirical trends in either dimension
Giving a both-sides answer without any position
followUps: Does high inequality within a country slow its overall development? What does the evidence suggest?

question: How has China's growing involvement in Africa and other developing regions changed the development landscape?
lookingFor: A balanced, evidence-based view of Chinese development finance — acknowledging both the infrastructure benefit and the debt and conditionality concerns.

bullets: Note that China has become a major source of development finance and infrastructure, outside Western institutions
Engage with the debt trap debate — where it holds and where it's been overstated
Reflect on what it means for development theory — is this South-South solidarity, pragmatic mercantilism, or something else?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: China's Belt and Road Initiative has financed significant infrastructure across Africa, Asia, and Latin America — roads, ports, railways that Western donors and institutions often wouldn't touch. For some governments that's a genuine alternative to conditionality-heavy IMF or World Bank lending. The 'debt trap diplomacy' narrative — that China deliberately engineers debt to seize assets — has been scrutinised and is probably overstated in its deliberate form; Zambia and Sri Lanka are complicated cases that don't fit a simple story. What I find more interesting is what Chinese involvement reveals about development theory: it's largely transactional, doesn't demand democratic governance or transparency standards, and is driven partly by Chinese resource needs and export markets. That's not entirely unlike Western development finance in earlier eras, which is uncomfortable to acknowledge.

commonMistakes: Accepting the debt trap narrative uncritically without noting the evidence debates
Ignoring the genuine infrastructure contribution and the demand for it
Treating Chinese involvement as uniquely predatory without context from historical Western finance
followUps: Should recipient governments accept Chinese loans on those terms? What's the alternative?

question: Microfinance was celebrated as a poverty-reduction breakthrough and later criticised heavily. What do you think explains that arc?
lookingFor: Familiarity with the microfinance story — the Grameen Bank, the scaling, and the randomised trial evidence — and an ability to explain why initial optimism gave way to scepticism.

bullets: Explain what microfinance promised and why it was initially celebrated
Point to what the RCT evidence actually showed — modest effects, not transformation
Identify structural reasons it couldn't close the gap between promise and outcome
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Microfinance promised to solve poverty by giving poor people — especially women — access to credit they'd been denied. The Grameen Bank's group lending model was genuinely innovative. The problem is that the story got ahead of the evidence, and when rigorous randomised trials finally ran in the 2000s and 2010s, the average impact was modest: consumption went up a little, some small businesses were supported, but there wasn't the transformative poverty escape the advocates promised. The structural critique that resonates with me is that credit without complementary inputs — skills, market access, infrastructure — can't solve problems caused by those missing inputs. In some cases, high interest rates and aggressive repayment led to debt spirals. The arc from hero to villain is partly about scaling a niche innovation beyond what it could do.

commonMistakes: Not knowing the Grameen Bank or Yunus connection at all
Dismissing microfinance entirely without acknowledging what it does accomplish for some borrowers
Confusing microfinance with micro-grants or cash transfer programmes
followUps: Do you think direct cash transfers are a better instrument than microloans? What does the evidence say?

question: What are the limitations of using income alone to measure poverty?
lookingFor: Awareness that poverty is multidimensional — and familiarity with alternative measures like the HDI or MPI.

bullets: Explain what income measures miss — health, education, dignity, security, voice
Reference at least one alternative measure and what it adds
Note that measurement choices are political, not just technical
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A dollar-a-day threshold tells you whether someone can buy a minimal consumption bundle, but it doesn't tell you whether they can read, whether their children will survive to age five, or whether they live in constant fear of violence. Amartya Sen and Mahbub ul Haq's Human Development Index was built precisely on the argument that income is a means, not an end — life expectancy and educational attainment matter independently. The Multidimensional Poverty Index goes further, mapping deprivations across health, education, and living standards, which reveals that people who are above the income line can still be intensely deprived in other dimensions. What I find important is that measurement choices are never neutral: if you measure income, income-raising policies look successful; if you measure capabilities, you might reach a different verdict on the same intervention.

commonMistakes: Only criticising income measurement without offering an alternative
Confusing the HDI with the MPI
Missing the political point that measurement shapes what counts as success
followUps: Is there such a thing as a purely objective poverty measure, or is it always a value judgement?

question: NGOs play a huge role in development. What are the risks of relying on them so heavily?
lookingFor: A critical but fair view of NGO-led development — not just praise for their mission, but awareness of accountability gaps, dependency, and the privatisation of public goods.

bullets: Acknowledge the genuine contribution NGOs make — reaching where governments can't or won't
Identify accountability gaps — NGOs answer to donors, not beneficiaries
Note the structural risk: heavy NGO presence can substitute for, rather than build, state capacity
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: NGOs can reach populations in fragile or hostile states and often work faster than government systems. But the accountability structure is almost backwards: they answer to Northern donors who fund them, not to the communities they serve. That shapes what programmes get run — what's fundable, not necessarily what's needed. There's also a deeper concern: in some contexts, NGOs have effectively privatised services like health and education, relieving pressure on states to build the systems that would outlast the grant cycle. I find the concept of the 'NGO-isation of development' a useful critique — it can entrench a model where communities are permanent recipients rather than rights-bearing citizens with claim on a capable state.

commonMistakes: Only celebrating NGOs' mission without any critical perspective
Treating all NGOs as identical — ignoring the range from Médecins Sans Frontières to small local organisations
No awareness of the donor accountability problem
followUps: How would you design better accountability mechanisms for international NGOs?

question: Amartya Sen famously argued that famines are political, not natural events. What did he mean, and do you agree?
lookingFor: Understanding of Sen's entitlements approach and its implication that food insecurity is about access and power, not absolute scarcity.

bullets: Explain the entitlements framework — food availability is not the same as food access
Give an example that supports the argument (e.g. Bengal famine or an African famine)
Assess where the argument might be complicated — e.g. climate shocks reducing total supply
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Sen's argument in Poverty and Famines is that famines rarely occur because there isn't enough food in aggregate — they occur because people lose their entitlements to food: their wages fall, their land is taken, food prices spike, or governments direct food away from affected populations. The 1943 Bengal famine happened during a period of food exports from India. More recent famines in Somalia and South Sudan have occurred in contexts of conflict deliberately used to deny food access. The implication is profound: solving famine requires addressing power, war, and market failures, not just food production. Where I'd add nuance is that climate shocks are now reducing harvests in ways that create scarcity beyond entitlement failures — the two dimensions are converging.

commonMistakes: Confusing Sen's argument with a simple claim that 'there's enough food in the world' (which misses the entitlements mechanism)
No concrete historical example
Accepting or rejecting the argument without engaging with it
followUps: What policy does Sen's framework point toward as a famine prevention strategy?

question: Is rapid urbanisation in the Global South a problem or an opportunity for development?
lookingFor: A nuanced view of urbanisation — not simply a crisis narrative, but engagement with agglomeration benefits and the inequality and infrastructure pressures it creates.

bullets: Identify the economic benefits of cities — agglomeration, labour market density, innovation
Acknowledge the risks — informality, slum growth, infrastructure stress
Argue that the outcome depends on governance and planning capacity
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Urbanisation has historically been one of the most reliable drivers of poverty reduction — cities concentrate economic activity, raise wages, and allow women in particular to access labour markets and education that rural areas often don't. So I resist the crisis framing. The challenge is that many Global South cities are growing faster than the governance capacity and infrastructure investment needed to manage that growth — the result is informal settlements with poor sanitation and insecure tenure, not slums as a permanent fate but as a consequence of underinvestment. Mike Davis's Planet of Slums is a vivid account of the risks. Whether urbanisation is an opportunity or a problem is largely a question of whether states can plan land, housing, and services fast enough.

commonMistakes: Treating urbanisation as simply a problem to be solved
Ignoring informal economies and the agency of urban migrants
No awareness of agglomeration economics
followUps: What's one policy that has genuinely improved outcomes in rapidly urbanising cities?

question: Which development thinker do you find most compelling, and why?
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual engagement with a specific thinker — the ability to summarise their contribution and say something critical about it.

bullets: Name a thinker with a specific argument, not just a name-drop
Explain why that argument is compelling to you personally
Identify at least one limitation or critique you'd make
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I keep coming back to Amartya Sen, particularly the capabilities approach he developed with Martha Nussbaum. The move from 'what do people have?' to 'what can people do and be?' feels like a genuine advance — it makes development about human flourishing rather than commodity accumulation. What draws me to it is the philosophical seriousness: it takes freedom as both an end and a means. My critique would be operational: the list of capabilities raises hard questions about who decides which capabilities matter and across what cultural contexts. There's a tension between the universalist ambition and respecting local self-determination. But it's a productive tension, and I'd rather have a framework with that problem than one that avoids human agency entirely.

commonMistakes: Naming a famous figure without engaging with what they actually argued
Uncritical hero-worship — no limitations or critique
Naming someone very obscure without being able to explain their work clearly
followUps: Is there a thinker whose work you find important even though you disagree with their conclusions?

question: Has globalisation been good or bad for the world's poorest people?
lookingFor: A data-aware, nuanced answer that moves beyond both the 'globalisation lifts all boats' narrative and the reflexive anti-globalisation critique.

bullets: Acknowledge the evidence that some regions — especially East and South Asia — have seen large poverty reductions alongside trade integration
Note who hasn't benefited — Sub-Saharan Africa, rural workers in middle-income countries, unskilled labour in rich countries
Argue that globalisation's effects are distributional, not uniform, and depend heavily on domestic policy
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The honest answer is that it's been deeply uneven. East Asian integration into global supply chains — particularly in manufacturing — accompanied real income growth for hundreds of millions of people in China, South Korea, Taiwan, and later Vietnam. That's a significant empirical fact that critics of globalisation can't dismiss. But Sub-Saharan Africa largely didn't experience those gains, and within fast-growing countries the distribution was skewed. Milanovic's analysis of global income distribution shows a squeezed middle — global poor and emerging Asian workers gained, but working classes in rich countries and the poorest of the global poor saw limited benefit. So 'good or bad' is the wrong frame: who gained, at what stage of development, under what domestic conditions — those are the right questions.

commonMistakes: Taking an unambiguous position without acknowledging the empirical complexity
No awareness of East Asian growth as a case study in either direction
Conflating trade liberalisation with globalisation as a whole
followUps: What would a 'better' globalisation look like? Is that politically achievable?

question: Is it ever appropriate for external actors to intervene in a country's domestic development path without invitation?
lookingFor: Structured ethical reasoning about sovereignty, paternalism, and the responsibility to protect — applied specifically to a development context.

bullets: Acknowledge the sovereignty norm and why it matters — especially given colonial history
Identify cases where the argument for intervention is strongest — e.g. atrocity prevention, but also consider conditional lending
Arrive at a principled position that accounts for power asymmetry
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The colonial history makes this question especially live in Development Studies — outside actors have a poor track record of 'knowing best' about other countries' development paths, and the concept has been used to justify a lot of extraction disguised as assistance. So my default is a strong presumption against uninvited intervention. Where the argument gets genuinely hard is in cases of mass atrocity or famine deliberately engineered by a government against its own people — there I think there's a case for intervention, though I'm aware it's been used selectively and politically. IMF structural adjustment conditionality is a kind of intervention that's less dramatic but probably more consequential over decades. I'd ask whether the intervening actor is accountable for what goes wrong and whether communities affected have meaningful voice — those seem like the right criteria.

commonMistakes: Either dismissing sovereignty entirely or treating it as absolute
No awareness of how 'development intervention' has historically served other interests
Failing to distinguish between different types of intervention — military, economic, humanitarian
followUps: Does your answer change if the intervention is requested by part of the population but resisted by the government?

subtopic: Career and application of the degree
question: Where do you see yourself applying a Development Studies degree in the longer term?
lookingFor: A direction that is grounded and connected to the course — not a fantasy career, but an honest account of what the degree opens up and why that matters to the applicant.

bullets: Name a sector or type of work — policy, research, civil society, journalism, international institutions
Connect it to a specific interest that runs through their application
Show awareness that the path isn't linear and that the degree builds analytical tools that apply broadly
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My honest answer is that I'm more confident about the problem I want to work on — land rights and displacement — than the exact institutional home. I could see myself in a research role, in policy analysis for a bilateral agency, or eventually with a community-based land rights organisation. What draws me to Development Studies is that it builds the analytical and methodological toolkit to engage with that problem seriously, rather than channelling me toward one sector. I know the path won't be linear, and I'm okay with that — I'd rather have a rigorous grounding in how to think about complex problems than a credential for a specific job title.

commonMistakes: Naming a specific prestigious institution with no substance behind it — 'I want to work at the UN'
A career goal with no connection to anything in the rest of their application
Sounding so vague that there's no genuine direction at all
followUps: What's one thing you're doing now that's building toward that path?
